EP.3 Learning JS From Zero — Day 3: Grabbing HTML Elements with Selectors
Day 3 of my reincarnated life learning JS. This time we figure out how to grab HTML elements, so I'm going to walk through selectors.
There are a bunch of ways to grab an HTML element, but two are the most common. One only lets you select by ID, the other lets you select by ID
className. Personally, in my own dev habits, I lean on selecting by ID most of the time. A selector usually ends up looking something like this:
Let's start with selecting by ID, which is the way I'm most used to. That's
language-js shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
So what shows up after you select an element with this? Let's toss the code onto CodePen, then type

element — also called a DOM element. You can picture a selector like you're someone with an Appraisal Eye skill: that appraisal eye is basically the selector, and you use it to inspect (select) other people's stats, values, and skills. Except this appraisal eye is super OP and can even borrow other people's skills, too (gets punched).
Next up is the other way of writing it. This one lets you use either an ID or a className as the selector, and if you've written jQuery before it'll probably feel familiar:

var boxId = document.querySelector('#box'); // select by ID, so prefix with #
var boxId = document.querySelector('.box'); // select by class, so use a .
// select by ID, so prefix with #
// select by class, so use a .
The result here is the same as just selecting by ID, so I won't bother demoing it. You might be wondering, "so should I use
If you want fuller support across all browsers, I'd personally go with

has really high support these days too — only some very old browsers don't handle it — so honestly it comes down to which selection style you prefer, pick one. But if your project's users tend to be on older browsers, then I'd lean toward
For my own dev habits, I'm more used to
— after all, an HTML ID is unique across the whole page.
Of course JavaScript gives you other ways to select DOM elements too. For example, there's a way to select by tagName:
The result is the same, naturally, but you'll see the structure looks a little different — kind of like an array:
has very high support too, so you can use it to select DOM as well. But generally it's more commonly used for selecting multiple tagNames at once:
